3  PURPOSE OF THIS DOCUMENT 

This document describes the tests and test results used for the assessment of NASA CrIS Level 1B (L1B) 
geolocated  radiance  product,  Version  3.  Several  important  changes  were  made  relative  to  the  earlier 
Version 2 product. The reader is referred to the Version 3 Product Users’ Guide (PUG) [8] for a description 
of changes to the CrIS L1B product compared to the earlier product. The reader is referred to the Version 
3 Delta ATBD [7] for a description of differences compared to the operational NOAA product algorithm, 
as described in the SDR ATBD [6].  

4  BACKGROUND 

The  Suomi  National  Polar-orbiting  Partnership  (SNPP)  satellite  was  the  intended  “bridge”  mission 
between the NASA Earth Observing System (EOS) satellites and the subsequent operational sensors of the 
Joint  Polar  Satellite  System  (JPSS),  a  joint  project  between  NOAA  and  NASA.  Following  a  period  of 
evaluation of algorithms supplied to the JPSS program for SNPP, NASA chose to support the development 
of  software  for  implementation  of  algorithms  provided  by  the  science  community.  The  SIPS  (Science 
Investigator-led Processing Systems) were established to support the various communities (Land, Ocean, 
Atmosphere, Sounder, etc.) by creating well-documented satellite products distributed through the NASA 
EOSDIS DAAC archives. The reprocessing of the data records by the DAACs ensures that SNPP and JPSS 
data can be used for climate studies as well as atmospheric process studies. 

10 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 5  EPOCHS 

The Cross-track Infrared Sounder on the SNPP satellite was launched 28 October 2011. A ninety day period 
after  launch  was  devoted  to  out-gassing  and  on-orbit  sensor  calibration.  Valid  L1B  data  periods  are 
described in the PUG [8]. The CrIS on the JPSS-1 satellite (subsequently renamed NOAA-20) was launched 
into  orbit  on  18  November  2017  and  followed  a  similar  on-orbit  checkout  to  SNPP  before  beginning 
routine operations. The design of the NASA CrIS L1B software allows for the post-processing of mission 
data from each CrIS instrument using common calibration methods with configuration files that contain 
parameters unique to each instrument. These parameter files contain time periods (called “epochs”) for 
which  the  calibration  of  the  sensor  is  considered  to  be  valid.  In  this  way  the  L1B  product  files  should 
contain only data for valid time periods, although the provided quality flags should be used to screen out 
spurious intermittent bad data. 

5.1  SNPP epoch definition 

The current SNPP epochs are delineated by changes between the redundant on-board electronics sides 
which were made in 2019 and 2021 due to failures in the digital signal processors. 

●  SNPP epoch 1 

o  April 2012 to 2019 side change (24 June 2019, 1546z) 
o  The  mid-wave  (MW)  band  dropped  out  at  a  certain  point,  with  potentially  other  data 

quality issues preceding the side change 

●  SNPP epoch 2 

o  2019 side change (24 June 2019, 1547z) to 2021 side change (12 July 2021, 1200z) 
o  The  long-wave  (LW)  band  dropped  out  at  a  certain  point,  with  potentially  other  data 

quality issues preceding the side change 

●  SNPP epoch 3 

o  2021 side change (14 July 2021, 0000z) onward 

5.2  NOAA-20 epoch definition 

●  NOAA-20 epoch 1 

o  Mission start up to current time on Side 1 

6  L1B PRODUCT RESOLUTION 

The  resolution  of  CrIS  data  as  received  from  the  SNPP  satellite  was  increased  several  years  into  the 
mission. It was decided to produce SNPP L1B products at two different resolutions, to meet the goals of 
providing  spectrally  consistent  products  with  the  longest  possible  duration  and  also  with  the  highest 
possible spectral resolution. These L1B products are referred to as Normal Spectral Resolution (NSR) and 
Full Spectral Resolution (FSR). 

The SNPP NSR product has a lower resolution in the short-wave and mid-wave spectra compared to the 
long-wave. The start date is 19 April 2012. The SNPP FSR product has the same spectral resolution in all 

11 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 three bands, and has a later start date of  2 November  2015. The  NOAA-20 FSR product has the same 
spectral resolution as the SNPP FSR product, and spans the entire NOAA-20 mission. 

For more information on product resolution, refer to the PUG [8].

8  TESTS 

The tests described in this document are individual analyses conducted to either provide an overview of 
product quality over the entire mission or are specifically intended to confirm new algorithm changes that 
appear for the first time in the new product version.

The tests performed for this report are guided by the experience of the authors of this report but should 
not  be  considered  comprehensive  nor  the  final  word  on  the  data  product  quality.  For  the  ongoing 
assessment of NASA CrIS L1B product quality we refer the reader to the scientific literature and to recent 
conference publications as appropriate. 

9  TEST RESULTS 

The results of the tests performed for the assessment of the NASA CrIS L1B product are described in the 
following subsections. 

9.1  Geolocation Assessment 

Purpose: Evaluate quality of geolocation 

Task:   Compare L1B v3 with the SDR (NOAA IDPS) CrIS product 

Method and Results: 

For each satellite and epoch, a time range was selected in which to directly compare geolocation from all 
CrIS L1B observations to corresponding observations from the NOAA IDPS CrIS SDR product. Time ranges 
were chosen to cover all latitudes and at least one half of an orbit. Statistics were computed for L1B versus 
SDR for: 

●  Difference in earth location at geoid surface, in meters 
●  Difference in sensor/viewing zenith and azimuth angles, in degrees 
●  Difference in solar zenith and azimuth angles, in degrees 

The following tables show the times and locations of the comparisons for versions 3.0.1 and 3.0.4 of the 
CrIS  L1B  software,  along  with  the  distribution  of  earth  location  differences  versus  the  SDR  product.  A 
known contributor to the location differences is that L1B geolocates to the earth’s geoid surface while 
SDR uses an ellipsoid model. The geoid model used for L1B is a more accurate representation of the earth’s 
geopotential surface, and can differ from the ellipsoid model by up to 100 meters.

technique for calculating the location of maximum sun glint, which is reported once per scan line in the 
L1B product. The version 3 glint calculation uses an ellipsoid earth model to replace the spherical model 
used in previous versions. This validation was performed by calculating the angular difference between 
the reflected sun-to-earth vector at the reported glint location and the earth-to-sensor vector. Ideally, 
these  vectors  would  have  the  same  direction  (and  thus  angular  difference  of  0).  This  process  was 
conducted for a handful of granules using both version 3.0.1 and the older version 2 CrIS L1B product. A 
typical test granule (start time 1 January 2019, 0230z) that had a mean angular difference of 0.178 degrees 
in version 2 improved to a mean angular difference of 0.012 degrees in version 3. 

9.2  FOV-2-FOV Spectral and Radiometric Calibration Consistency  

Purpose: Evaluate relative biases of the CrIS 3x3 fields of view (FOVs) within a field of regard (FOR) for 
spectral and radiometric accuracy and stability. 

Task:  

FOV-2-FOV radiometric and spectral comparisons over months of data for SNPP FSR and NSR and NOAA-
20  FSR.  Check  that  FOV-2-FOV  relative  spectral  calibration  is  less  than  1ppm  and  relative  radiometric 
calibration is order 0.1 and quantify the differences over time. 

Method and Results: 

Step 1: For each L1B granule (6 minutes, 45 scan lines, 30 fields of regard in cross-track), a subset of FORs 
are selected within the granule that satisfy the following criteria: 

(1)  FOR number within a specified range near nadir,  
(2)  900 cm-1 channel radiance standard deviation over 3x3 < X radiance units 

For  the  subset  of  selected  FORs  within  a  granule  the  mean  radiance  and  standard  deviation  over  the 
granule is computed for each Field of View (FOV). 

Step 2: The relative spectral shift between FOVs is computed for each granule using the mean spectra 
from Step 1 with the center FOV (FOV5) as the reference. The root-mean-square (RMS) method is used 
which determines the value of a wavenumber scale factor applied to CrIS mean spectra which minimizes 
the square root of the standard deviation of FOVX-FOV5 spectral channels over a pre-specified spectral 
region in each band. The spectral ranges used are the following:     

Long-wave (LW):  
Mid-wave (MW):  
Short-wave (SW):   

730 - 740 cm-1 
1350 - 1380 cm-1 
2236 - 2248 cm-1 

Step 3: The relative radiometric bias is computed for each granule using the mean FOV spectra computed 
in Step 1 and with FOV5 as the reference. This is a simple difference. 

16 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 Step 4: A daily estimate is created from the individual granule estimates over user specified latitude ranges 
and solar zenith angles and ocean/land masks. The median function is used to create the daily estimate 
to be less sensitive to outlier values. The daily estimates are found to have a very good reproducibility 
from day to day which makes them suitable for long-term trending.

9.4  Clear Sky Observation Minus Calculation Assessment 

Purpose:  
model calculations using best estimates of the atmospheric thermodynamic state and gas composition. 

Assess the CrIS radiance product spectral and radiometric quality using radiative transfer 

Tasks:    

(1)  Compute observation minus calculation residuals for statistically significant datasets 
(2)  Compute SNPP and NOAA-20 CrIS double differences  
(3)  Compute excessive spectral ringing estimates 

Method and Results: 

Step 1:  Selection of uniform CrIS scenes using the standard deviation of the observed 900 cm-1 window 
brightness temperature within each 3x3 field of regard over the ocean at night only. Subselect full FORs 
for  which  the  900  cm-1  BT  observation  is  within  a  specified  threshold  from  a  forecast  model  skin 
temperature (use a threshold of 2 - 3 K difference).  

Step  2:  Creation  of  atmospheric  state  profile  using  ECMWF  forecast  model  fields  provided  by  NOAA 
combined with best estimates of minor gas and heavy molecule concentrations and ocean emissivity. 

Step 3a: Compute line-by-line top of atmosphere radiances using either LBLRTM and reduce them to CrIS 
spectral resolution OR 

28 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 Step 3b: Compute the fast model OSS at IASI spectral resolution and reduce to CrIS spectral resolution. 

Step 4: Compute mean brightness temperature spectral Obs-Calc differences for specified latitude and 
time periods. 

Step 5: Compute double difference between SNPP and NOAA-20 CrIS using consistent time periods and 
methods. 

For v3.0.1 

LBLRTM calculations were performed for over a dozen SNPP and NOAA-20 case study L1B granules, each 
with  significant  uniform,  clear  sky  scenes.  For  all  SNPP  and  NOAA-20  case  studies,  obs-calc  biases  are 
generally well under +/- 1K in the LW, +/- 2K in the MW, and +/- 2K in the SW. These biases are largely a 
result  of  atmospheric  state  errors  in  the  forward  modeling  and  can  be  anticipated.  Excessive  spectral 
ringing [11] is estimated by a double difference of the [observation minus apodized observation] minus 
the [calculation minus apodized calculation], and is illustrated in Figure  9.4-1 for an example NOAA-20 
granule. For both SNPP and NOAA-20 this quantity is overall well under 0.5 K magnitude for the LW and 
MW, while in the SW this quantity is overall well under 1 K across the bands; the upper bounds of those 
numeric ranges are only reached in limited spectral regions as highlighted in Figure 9.4-1. These results 
show consistent improvement  upon previous  L1B version analyses and are true  for both  NSR and FSR 
products. Results for an example NOAA-20 granule from 24 July 2021 are shown in Figure 9.4-1. It should 
be noted that the features present in the excessive spectral ringing estimates include not only excessive 
spectral ringing but also detailed spectral line differences between the observation and calculation. For 
example, structures present near the band edges are excessive spectral ringing (e.g. the LW 650 cm-1 band 
edge), but where carbon monoxide is an absorber at <2200 cm-1 in the SW the features present are due

spectral ringing and spectral features of some regions cannot be distinguished, yet this metric remains 
useful to assess excessive spectral ringing changes between calibration versions as well as in an absolute

 9.8  Earth Rotation Doppler Effect Correction Assessment 

Purpose:  
3 product. 

Assess the Earth rotation Doppler effect correction, which was introduced in the Version 

Task:   

Evaluate the size (and sign) of the Doppler correction for Earth-only rotation. 

Method and Results: 

The relative motion of the CrIS sensor with respect to the atmosphere causes an apparent shift in the 
observed CrIS spectrum. This relative motion can be decomposed into two parts; 1) rotation of the planet 
around an axis running between the north and south poles as the satellite follows a nearly pole-to-pole 
orbit, and 2) the forward motion of the satellite along the orbit. The Earth-only Doppler effect is largest 
at the equator and is nearly zero at the poles. The satellite velocity Doppler effect is constant along the 
orbit but only for fields of view with a view angle that has a component along the direction of satellite 
motion. 

A method for detection of a Doppler shift induced in CrIS radiance spectra due to the Earth’s rotation was 
described in Chen et al [10]. 

For this test report, we compare v3.0.0-beta6 (without Doppler correction) to v3.0.0-beta8 (with Earth-
only Doppler correction)  using radiative transfer model calculations as a  reference.  The RMS Absolute 
Spectral Calibration Method was used to determine the spectral shift (in PPM) of each detector band in 
each field of view (1-9) and each field of regard (1-30). 

Note that the Doppler Shift caused by the satellite velocity relative to the Earth’s atmosphere was not 
implemented in CrIS L1B v3 and is not evaluated in this test report. 

For v3.0.1 

The Earth-only Doppler correction implemented in L1B v3.0.1 is assessed in Figures 9.8-1 and 9.8-2. The 
magnitude of the full Doppler effect which was not corrected in v3 is shown in Figure 9.8-3. The remaining 
effects could be corrected in a future CrIS L1B version, however that should be done in conjunction with 
a refinement of the CrIS spectral calibration for each field of view.

9.9  Lunar Intrusion Detection Assessment  

Purpose:  

Assess the lunar intrusion detection algorithm 

Task:   

The  moon  occasionally  enters  the  CrIS  Deep  Space  (DS)  view  under  normal  operating  conditions.  The 
purpose  of  the  lunar  intrusion  detection  algorithm  is  to  identify  Deep  Space  views  that  contain  a 
radiometrically significant lunar contribution to the observed radiance. If lunar intrusion is detected, the 
DS spectrum is marked as invalid and excluded from the moving window average. This results in a reduced 
number of DS spectra in the sliding window average used in the calibration of the current observation, 
and the Lunar Intrusion Detected flag is set for the observation.

Compare lunar intrusion as indicated in CrIS L1B quality flags to IDPS CLASS SDR and to a previous version. 
Identify any non-physical patterns.

9.11  Imaginary Part Quality Check Assessment 

Purpose:  

Assess the imaginary part quality flag 

Task:   

A large imaginary component of the calibrated radiance is an indication of poor quality of the calibrated 
radiance. A set of thresholds have been defined and are used to quality check the imaginary part of the 
calibrated radiance. When thresholds are exceeded the imaginary radiance anomaly flag is set. 

Method and Results: 

Regression test the v3 results against v2. Since the v3 thresholds have been refined and are tighter than 
the v2 thresholds, the expectation is that more radiances will be flagged (fewer false negatives).  Time 
periods with a greater than nominal frequency of flagged  radiances are spot-checked. Results are also 
compared to the IDPS-processed SDRs available through CLASS.

9.12  Radiometric Uncertainty Assessment 

Purpose: Assess the ability to generate Radiometric Uncertainty for any radiance (or granule) for the v3 
product. 

Task:   

As described by Tobin et al 2013, the Radiometric Uncertainty (RU) of the Cross-track Infrared Sounder 
(CrIS) characterizes the accuracy of the observed radiance spectra [2, 3, 4, 5]. RU represents an upper limit 
of the bias with respect to the true radiance and does not include effects such as detector noise which 
vary  randomly  from  one  spectrum  to  another.  The  radiometric  uncertainty  is  scene  and  instrument 
environment dependent. However, it is possible to accurately estimate the radiometric uncertainty for 
any CrIS NASA L1B calibrated radiance using the radiance data, auxiliary data provided in a Version 3 L1B 
granule, and a small amount of ancillary information (provided in a static file that is available on request 
and documented in the CrIS L1B Radiometric Uncertainty Description Document [9]). This allows the end-
user  to  determine  the  radiometric  uncertainty  for  any  NASA  CrIS  L1B  radiance  without  the  explicit 
calculation  of  the  combined  uncertainty  for  the  CrIS  radiometric  calibration  which  would  require  the 
corresponding  L1a  data  including  any  context  granules  used  in  the  calibration  and  the  complete  L1B 
processing  package.  The  CrIS  L1B  Radiometric  Uncertainty  Description  Document  [9]  describes  the 
procedure to estimate the CrIS radiometric uncertainty for any CrIS NASA L1B radiance. 

64 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 Method and Results: 

The radiometric uncertainty for a random granule is calculated following the procedure and example code 
described  in  the  CrIS  L1B  Radiometric  Uncertainty  Description  Document  [9].  The  result  is  compared 
versus the explicit calculation of the combined uncertainty for the CrIS radiometric calibration using the 
corresponding L1a data (including context granules) and the complete L1B processing package. 

Result: 
The results verify that the procedure and example code described in the CrIS L1B Radiometric Uncertainty 
Description Document produce an accurate radiometric uncertainty evaluation for the CrIS sensor [9]. An 
example for a single SNPP granule is shown in Figure 9.12-1. 

Figure 9.12- 1 Mean radiometric uncertainty for a single SNPP granule for a scene containing scattered cloud over tropical ocean 
(01 April 2018, 2206z). The mean brightness temperature spectrum is shown in the top row with the 3-σ uncertainty contributors 
and total uncertainty shown in the middle row, and the total 3-σ plotted versus scene temperature and colored by wavenumber 
in the bottom row.  The LW results are shown in the left column, the MW results in the middle column, and the SW results in the 
right column. 

10 TESTING SUMMARY 

The  NASA  CrIS  L1B  v3  product  incorporates  several  important  changes  relative  to  CrIS  L1B  v2.  These 
include the addition of a correction for scene mirror polarization which primarily affects cold brightness 
temperature and short wavelengths, the correction for a spectral Doppler shift in the observed spectra 
caused by the Earth rotation relative to the satellite, improvements in the lunar and spike detection, and 
the addition of auxiliary variables needed for the computation of radiometric uncertainty. Various tests 
65 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 are described to document the assessment that was performed by the CrIS L1B team prior to and following 
the release of the CrIS L1B product version by NASA Sounder SIPS. The same NASA CrIS L1B v3 software 
was applied to both SNPP and NOAA-20 CrIS data in order to create a consistently processed NASA CrIS 
L1B data record across both missions, with the exception of a minor software update that was released 
to process SNPP data after the 2021 side change. Small differences between the SNPP and NOAA-20 CrIS 
products are shown to be consistent with on-orbit radiometric uncertainty estimates of each sensor.
